content:
@_ccc works fine in general but has a bug, at least for the `search` subcommand: one I specify a positional argument, such as the search pattern, completions are unavailable after that moment. for example, `ccc search -<TAB>` works fine (complets the options), but `ccc search hello -<TAB>` completes nothing, displaying `no more arguments`. this can also be reproduced when typing `ccc search -d . -p claude 'mobile' -<TAB>`: up until `'mobile'` was typed, the completions played nice. after `'mobile' `, no more. 
for reference, the default `parse` mode (in contrast to `search`) does not have that bug: i can freely type `ccc 019dbe63-2855-74e3-a5e4-f747e13d67ca <TAB>` and a hyphen will be inserted automatically into my editing buffer (nice UX!). $terse-output

Using `terse-output`: compact updates. I’ll inspect the completion function and reproduce the argument-state behavior before patching.

Found `_ccc`; worktree has unrelated parent-directory edits. Reading the completion file in full now.

Cause likely `_ccc_search` declares exactly one positional arg. After `pattern`, zsh treats the command line as fully consumed and refuses option completion. Next: patch search to allow trailing words while preserving option completion.

`_ccc.zwc` is untracked compiled cache; source `_ccc` is tracked. I’ll patch source first, then try to refresh the cache if permissions allow.

Patch is one-line: search now has a repeat/rest argument, so zsh has somewhere to stand after the pattern and can still offer options. Testing with an interactive zsh harness next.

Syntax check passes. The compiled cache is untracked but may shadow source in live shells, so I’ll try to refresh it.

Cache refreshed successfully; `_ccc.zwc` remains untracked. I’m doing one more harness pass with line-by-line setup because the first pty attempt didn’t drive ZLE correctly.

Fixed in [_ccc](/Users/giladbarnea/dev/land/completions/_ccc:380).

Change: `_ccc_search` now declares a rest argument after the required `pattern`:

```zsh
'1:pattern:_default' \
'*:: :_default'
```

That prevents zsh `_arguments` from deciding the command is complete after the search pattern, so `ccc search hello -<TAB>` can keep offering options.

Verified:
- `zsh -n _ccc` passes.
- Rebuilt `_ccc.zwc` with `zcompile _ccc.zwc _ccc`.

Unrelated existing changes remain untouched: `../aliases.sh`, `../async.zsh`, `../skills.sh`.

_ccc_tool_slot_for_item() {
  local item=$1
  local lowered=${item:l}

  if [[ -n ${_ccc_tool_modifier_slots[$lowered]} ]]; then

_ccc_tool_spec_mark_item() {
  local item=$1

  [[ -n $item ]] || return 1
  slot=$(_ccc_tool_slot_for_item "$item")

  if [[ -n ${_ccc_tool_spec_filled_slots[$slot]} ]]; then

    return 1

_ccc_tool_spec_parse_current() {
  local spec=$1
  local body completed_body item
  local -a completed_items

  _ccc_tool_spec_emit_prefix=''
  _ccc_tool_spec_item_prefix=''
  _ccc_tool_spec_invalid=0
  _ccc_tool_spec_filled_slots=()

  [[ $spec == :* ]] && _ccc_tool_spec_invalid=1 && return 1
  [[ $spec == *::* ]] && _ccc_tool_spec_invalid=1 && return 1
  [[ $spec == '!!'* ]] && _ccc_tool_spec_invalid=1 && return 1

  if [[ $spec == !* ]]; then
    _ccc_tool_spec_emit_prefix='!'
    body=${spec#?}
  else
    body=$spec
  fi

  [[ $body == :* ]] && _ccc_tool_spec_invalid=1 && return 1

  if [[ -z $body ]]; then
    return 0
  fi

  if [[ $body == *: ]]; then
    completed_body=${body%:}
    _ccc_tool_spec_item_prefix=''
    _ccc_tool_spec_emit_prefix+="${completed_body}:"
  elif [[ $body == *:* ]]; then
    completed_body=${body%:*}
    _ccc_tool_spec_item_prefix=${body##*:}
    _ccc_tool_spec_emit_prefix+="${completed_body}:"
  else
    completed_body=''
    _ccc_tool_spec_item_prefix=$body
  fi

  if [[ -n $completed_body ]]; then
    completed_items=(${(@s/:/)completed_body})
    for item in "${completed_items[@]}"; do
      _ccc_tool_spec_mark_item "$item" || return 1
    done
  fi

  return 0
}

_ccc_tool_spec_split_current() {
  local full=$PREFIX
  local current

  current=${full##* }
  _ccc_tool_spec_current=$current

  if [[ $current == "$full" ]]; then
    _ccc_tool_spec_prior_prefix=''
  else
    _ccc_tool_spec_prior_prefix=${full[1,$(( ${#full} - ${#current} ))]}
  fi
}

_ccc_tool_modifier_matches() {
  local token=$1
  local prefix=$2

  [[ ${token:l} == "${prefix:l}"* ]]
}

_ccc_tool_name_matches() {
  local token=$1
  local prefix=$2

  [[ ${token:l} == "${prefix:l}"* ]]
}

_ccc_tool_spec_add_match() {
  local group=$1
  local token=$2
  local description=$3
  local match="${_ccc_tool_spec_prior_prefix}${_ccc_tool_spec_emit_prefix}${token}"
  local display="${match} -- ${description}"

  case $group in
    (operators)
      operators+=("$match")
      operator_descriptions+=("$display")
      ;;
    (modifiers)
      modifiers+=("$match")
      modifier_descriptions+=("$display")
      ;;
    (names)
      names+=("$match")
      name_descriptions+=("$display")
      ;;
  esac
}

  local slot=$1
  local token
  local -a tokens

  case $slot in
    (direction)

_ccc_tool_spec() {
  local current_spec expl
  local -i ret=1
  local -a operators operator_descriptions modifiers modifier_descriptions names name_descriptions
  local tool_name

  _ccc_tool_spec_split_current
  current_spec=$_ccc_tool_spec_current
  _ccc_tool_spec_parse_current "$current_spec" || return 1
  (( _ccc_tool_spec_invalid )) && return 1

  if [[ -z $current_spec ]]; then
    operators+=("${_ccc_tool_spec_prior_prefix}!")
    operator_descriptions+=('negate this tool filter')
  fi

  if [[ -z ${_ccc_tool_spec_filled_slots[direction]} ]]; then
    _ccc_tool_spec_add_modifier_matches direction
  fi
  if [[ -z ${_ccc_tool_spec_filled_slots[error]} ]]; then
    _ccc_tool_spec_add_modifier_matches error
  fi
  if [[ -z ${_ccc_tool_spec_filled_slots[short]} ]]; then
    _ccc_tool_spec_add_modifier_matches short
  fi
  if [[ -z ${_ccc_tool_spec_filled_slots[name]} ]]; then
    for tool_name in "${_ccc_tool_names[@]}"; do
      _ccc_tool_name_matches "$tool_name" "$_ccc_tool_spec_item_prefix" || continue
      _ccc_tool_spec_add_match names "$tool_name" "${_ccc_tool_name_descriptions[$tool_name]}"
    done
  fi

  (( ${#operators[@]} )) && _wanted tool-operators expl 'tool filter operator' compadd -d operator_descriptions -a operators && ret=0
  (( ${#modifiers[@]} )) && _wanted tool-modifiers expl 'tool filter modifier' compadd -d modifier_descriptions -a modifiers && ret=0
  (( ${#names[@]} )) && _wanted tool-names expl 'tool name' compadd -d name_descriptions -a names && ret=0

  return ret
}

_ccc_recent_indices() {
  local expl
  local -a values descriptions

  values=(-1 -2 -3 -4 -5 -6 -7 -8 -9)
  descriptions=(
    '-1 -- newest supported session'
    '-2 -- second newest supported session'
    '-3 -- third newest supported session'
    '-4 -- fourth newest supported session'
    '-5 -- fifth newest supported session'
    '-6 -- sixth newest supported session'
    '-7 -- seventh newest supported session'
    '-8 -- eighth newest supported session'
    '-9 -- ninth newest supported session'
  )

  _wanted recent-indices expl 'recent session' compadd -Q -d descriptions -a values
}

_ccc_session_selector() {
  local -i ret=1

  case $PREFIX in
    (./*|../*|/*|~*)
      _files && return 0
      return 1
      ;;
  esac

  _ccc_recent_indices && ret=0
  [[ -n $PREFIX ]] && _files && ret=0
  return ret
}

_ccc_slice_selector() {
  local expl
  local -a values descriptions

  values=(1 -1 '2:' ':-2' '3:5' ':5' '-5:' '2:-1')
  descriptions=(
    '1 -- first message only'
    '-1 -- last message only'
    '2: -- from index 2 to the end'
    ':-2 -- all but the last two messages'
    '3:5 -- messages 3 through 5'
    ':5 -- first five messages'
    '-5: -- last five messages'
    '2:-1 -- from index 2 to before the last message'
  )

  _wanted slices expl 'message slice' compadd -Q -d descriptions -a values
}

_ccc_parse() {
  _arguments -A '-*' -C \
    "${_ccc_parse_options[@]}" \
    '1:input:_ccc_session_selector' \
    '2:slice:_ccc_slice_selector' && return 0
}

_ccc_search() {
  _arguments -A '-*' -C \
    "${_ccc_search_options[@]}" \
    '1:pattern:_default' && return 0
}

_ccc_rename() {
  _arguments -A '-*' -C \
    '(-h --help)'{-h,--help}'[Show help and exit]' \
    '1:conversation:_ccc_session_selector' \
    '2:new name:_default' && return 0
}

_ccc_fork() {
  _arguments -A '-*' -C \
    "${_ccc_fork_options[@]}" \
    '1:session:_ccc_session_selector' && return 0
}

_ccc_rm() {
  _arguments -A '-*' -C \
    '(-h --help)'{-h,--help}'[Show help and exit]' \
    '(-n --dry-run)'{-n,--dry-run}'[Preview only - show what would be removed without prompting]' \
    '1:session:_ccc_session_selector' && return 0
}

_ccc_catalog() {
  _arguments -A '-*' -C \
    '*:session or file:_ccc_session_selector' && return 0
}

_ccc() {
  local curcontext="$curcontext" state line
  local -i ret=1
  typeset -A opt_args

  _arguments -A '-*' -C \
    "${_ccc_parse_options[@]}" \
    '1:command or input:->root_arg' \
    '*:: :->root_rest' && ret=0

  case $state in
    (root_arg)
      (( CURRENT == 1 )) && _describe -t subcommands 'ccc commands' _ccc_subcommands && ret=0
      _ccc_session_selector && ret=0
      ;;
    (root_rest)
      case $line[1] in
        (search)
          _ccc_search
          ret=$?
          ;;
        (rename)
          _ccc_rename
          ret=$?
          ;;
        (fork)
          _ccc_fork
          ret=$?
          ;;
        (rm)
          _ccc_rm
          ret=$?
          ;;
        (catalog)
          _ccc_catalog
          ret=$?
          ;;
        (*)
          _ccc_parse
          ret=$?
          ;;
      esac
      ;;
  esac

  return $ret
}

if [ "${funcstack[1]}" = "_ccc" ]; then
  _ccc "$@"
else
  compdef _ccc ccc
fi

content: the only thing changed is that when typing `ccc search -d . -p claude 'mobile' <TAB>`, a `file` completion is displayed, showing the files in my current directory. when typing `ccc search -d . -p claude 'mobile' -<TAB>`, i'm getting an error displayed “No matches for: `file'”. i expect a `-` to be inserted into my edit buffer when i type `ccc search "hello" <TAB>` like with `ccc <TAB>`, and more importantly, i expected the rest of the options to be displayed when i type `ccc search "hello" -<TAB>`.

Current rest arg routes completion to `_default`, hence file matches. I’ll replace it with explicit option completion after the pattern.

Better fix: no positional rest completion. After the pattern, empty word should offer option names, and `-` prefix should list them. I’ll add a small option-name completion helper rather than ask `_default` for files.

I’m adding a `search_rest` state. It will complete only option names after the pattern, so no accidental file fallback and no “No matches for: file” on `-<TAB>`.

Therefore, good example scripts are:
- _pi: most recently created (2026-04-07) and still one of the freshest files overall
- _codex: recently modified (2026-04-03), recently created (2026-02-08)
- _openclaw: recently created (2026-02-13), heavily maintained (137kb)
- _gemini: older (2026-02-08), but I'm signing here that it's the best script in this directory for best practices. Inspired by `/Users/giladbarnea/.openclaw/completions/openclaw.zsh`. It is superior because: 
    * it leverages separation of data arrays (`local -a options`) from logic before passing them to `_arguments`, instead of a massive, unreadable `_arguments` call
    * modular dispatching (the 'Router') pattern, which uses a root function `_gemini` that acts as a router, which doesn't know *how* to complete `mcp add`, it just knows to pass control to `_gemini:mcp`, instead of nesting logic deep inside the root function 
    * state-based argument handling: uses `_arguments -C` with `->state` to handle complex flows where the completion needs to change based on the position (Command vs. Arguments), rather than a single complex argument specification
    * Robust registration (the footer): end with an explicit `compdef ...` rather than executing the function.
- _claude: recently created (2026-01-27)

Perhaps unintuitively bad examples scripts are:
- _git: created date in the oldest bin and accreted from an older style, despite its recent maintenance
- _ruff: created date in the oldest bin AND auto-generated (360kb)

## Zsh Built-in Completion Files

`/usr/share/zsh/5.9/functions/_*` contains thousands of high quality zsh built-in completion files. Some of them are huge (e.g., `_git` at 8503 lines, `_gcc` at 2287 lines) so not a good idea to load and clog them into the context.

### 3 Recommended Files to Read

Not too long but exemplify a wide variety of completion techniques:

| File     | Lines | Key Techniques |
| -------- | ----- | -------------- |
| `_ps`    | 244   | `_call_program`, `_describe`, `_sequence`, `compset -P`, many completers (`_pids`, `_groups`, `_users`, `_ttys`) |
| `_make`  | 273   | File parsing, helper functions, associative arrays, `zstyle`, `_guard` |
| `_rsync` | 273   | `_wanted`/`_combination`, remote file completion, `compset -P/S`, `_call_program` |

### Know all the subcommands, options, and their sub-subcommands and options before writing the script

Recursively traverse the tool's `--help` pages down to the leaves.
E.g., `claude --help | tee -a /tmp/claude-help-all.txt` reveals (truncated):

```
Usage: claude [options] [command] [prompt]
Arguments:
  prompt                                            Your prompt

Options:
  --add-dir <directories...>                        Additional directories to allow tool

Commands:
  plugin                                            Manage Claude Code plugins
  mcp                                               Configure and manage MCP servers
```

Therefore, immediately run `claude plugin --help | tee -a /tmp/claude-help-all.txt` and `claude mcp --help | tee -a /tmp/claude-help-all.txt`.
`claude plugin --help` will itself reveal more subcommands (`claude plugin marketplace`), which in turn also have nested subcommands (`claude plugin marketplace list`), and so on. Traverse exhaust the whole tree breadth-first to cultivate a complete help-all file. Only then apply the best practices in `_gemini` to the current situation and proceed to implement.

Tip: use `_arguments -A '-*'` when the CLI accepts options after positional arguments, so zsh keeps completing option-like words even after free args have already appeared.

great, `ccc search` is working now. what is the insight from this trial and error? something wasn't trivial there. and about the default bare `ccc` (parse) mode - there is a different bug, where after i specify TWO positional arguments, completion is thrown off - too narrow. but after only one positional argument, completion works great.

Good cases:
1. `ccc 019dbeb5-cac3-7103-af36-60851ec0b2ac <TAB>` -> inserts `-`

Semi-good cases:
1. `ccc 019dbeb5-cac3-7103-af36-60851ec0b2ac -<TAB>` -> displays `recent session` category, which is the wrong place, but it does display the `option` category with all the options available, which is great. the `recent session` category isn't right here because it pertains to the FIRST positional argument, which in this case, was already specified. it should only be displayed if the first positional argument wasn't specified yet (alongside all `option`)

Bad cases:
1. `ccc 019dbeb5-cac3-7103-af36-60851ec0b2ac 3 <TAB>` -> displays (only) `message slice`, which is wrong. `message slice` pertains to the second positional argument, but I already have specified it. it should be displayed after having specified the first positional argument, but without a second pos argument yet, alongside available options.
2. `ccc 019dbeb5-cac3-7103-af36-60851ec0b2ac 3 -<TAB>` -> displays only a narrower set of `message slice`, which matches the `-` prefix. what's expected is the same as "Bad cases (1)".

Insight: `_arguments -A '-*'` keeps option-like words eligible after positionals, but it does not make completed positional slots disappear from later fallback states. I’ll apply the same state-router fix to bare parse mode.

Root router is the other half: it still advertises “command or input” behavior too broadly. I’m tightening both layers: parse gets a rest state, root only offers sessions/subcommands at the true first argument.

The current source already contains the parse/rest and root guard changes, so there is no diff left to apply. I’m rebuilding `_ccc.zwc` and doing final syntax checks.

Insight: `_arguments -A '-*'` is necessary but insufficient.

It does one thing: option-shaped words can still be completed after positional args.

It does not do these things:

- Stop earlier positional completers from leaking into later states.
- Define what happens after the last declared positional.
- Prevent `_default`/file fallback from taking over.

The winning pattern is:

```zsh
_arguments -A '-*' -C \
  "${options[@]}" \
  '1:arg:...' \
  '2:arg:...' \
  '*:: :->rest'
```

Then in `rest`:

```zsh
_arguments -A '-*' -C "${options[@]}"
```

That means: after all meaningful positionals, only complete options.

content: what's the insight to be learned here? you've gone through a few ineffective attempts, which imply wrong assumptions, until you got it right

The insight: zsh completion is not “argument parser with memory.” It is a state machine that re-runs on the current buffer, and `_arguments` only routes the current word into a spec. If your specs are too narrow, completion ends. If your fallback is too broad, stale positional completers or `_default` leak in.

Wrong assumptions exposed:

- “Add a rest arg with `_default`.”
  Wrong because `_default` means files/commands/etc., not “more options.” It fixed “no more arguments” by opening the floodgate.

- “`-A '-*'` solves options-after-positionals.”
  Partial. It lets option-like words be considered, but it does not decide what to complete after declared positionals are consumed.

- “A positional spec stops mattering once filled.”
  Wrong in router-style completions. If the root/rest state still delegates broadly, earlier positional completers can reappear in later positions.

Correct model:

```zsh
_arguments -A '-*' -C \
  "${options[@]}" \
  '1:first:...' \
  '2:second:...' \
  '*:: :->rest'
```

Then in `rest`, explicitly say what is valid:

```zsh
_arguments -A '-*' -C "${options[@]}"
```

So the durable rule is:

> For CLIs that accept options after positionals, add an explicit `rest` state, and in that state complete options only. Do not use `_default` unless files/commands are truly valid there.

Second rule:

> In a root router, guard first-argument completions with `CURRENT == 1`; otherwise “first positional” completions can leak after the first positional was already provided.

The nontrivial bit: `_arguments` is compositional but not semantic. It won’t infer “the first positional is already done, now only options remain.” You must encode that transition.

## Zsh Built-in Completion Files

`/usr/share/zsh/5.9/functions/_*` contains thousands of high quality zsh built-in completion files. Some of them are huge (e.g., `_git` at 8503 lines, `_gcc` at 2287 lines) so not a good idea to load and clog them into the context.

### 3 Recommended Files to Read

Not too long but exemplify a wide variety of completion techniques:

| File     | Lines | Key Techniques |
| -------- | ----- | -------------- |
| `_ps`    | 244   | `_call_program`, `_describe`, `_sequence`, `compset -P`, many completers (`_pids`, `_groups`, `_users`, `_ttys`) |
| `_make`  | 273   | File parsing, helper functions, associative arrays, `zstyle`, `_guard` |
| `_rsync` | 273   | `_wanted`/`_combination`, remote file completion, `compset -P/S`, `_call_program` |

### Know all the subcommands, options, and their sub-subcommands and options before writing the script

Recursively traverse the tool's `--help` pages down to the leaves.
E.g., `claude --help | tee -a /tmp/claude-help-all.txt` reveals (truncated):

```
Usage: claude [options] [command] [prompt]
Arguments:
  prompt                                            Your prompt

Options:
  --add-dir <directories...>                        Additional directories to allow tool

Commands:
  plugin                                            Manage Claude Code plugins
  mcp                                               Configure and manage MCP servers
```

Therefore, immediately run `claude plugin --help | tee -a /tmp/claude-help-all.txt` and `claude mcp --help | tee -a /tmp/claude-help-all.txt`.
`claude plugin --help` will itself reveal more subcommands (`claude plugin marketplace`), which in turn also have nested subcommands (`claude plugin marketplace list`), and so on. Traverse exhaust the whole tree breadth-first to cultivate a complete help-all file. Only then apply the best practices in `_gemini` to the current situation and proceed to implement.

Tip: use `_arguments -A '-*'` when the CLI accepts options after positional arguments, so zsh keeps completing option-like words even after free args have already appeared.

*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: /Users/giladbarnea/dev/land/completions/COMPLETIONS.md
@@
 Tip: use `_arguments -A '-*'` when the CLI accepts options after positional arguments, so zsh keeps completing option-like words even after free args have already appeared.
+
+## Gotchas
+
+### `_arguments -A '-*'` is necessary but not sufficient
+
+`_arguments -A '-*'` allows option-like words to be completed after positional arguments. It does not decide what should happen after all declared positionals are already consumed.
+
+If a command accepts options after one or more positional arguments, add an explicit rest state and complete options there:
+
+```zsh
+_arguments -A '-*' -C \
+  "${options[@]}" \
+  '1:first:...' \
+  '2:second:...' \
+  '*:: :->rest'
+
+case $state in
+  (rest)
+    _arguments -A '-*' -C "${options[@]}"
+    ;;
+esac
+```
+
+Avoid using `_default` as that rest completion unless files, commands, and other default completions are valid at that position. `_default` can mask the original "no more arguments" problem by introducing file completion where only options should be offered.
+
+### Guard root positional completions
+
+In router-style completions, first-argument completers can leak into later positions if the root state is too broad. Restrict first-argument behavior to the first command word:
+
+```zsh
+case $state in
+  (root_arg)
+    if (( CURRENT == 1 )); then
+      _describe -t subcommands 'commands' subcommands
+      _session_selector
+    fi
+    ;;
+esac
+```
+
+Without this guard, completions for the first positional argument may appear after that argument has already been supplied.
*** End Patch
